back_translation: When Jesus and his disciples came near to Jerusalem, arriving at the villages of Bethphage and Bethany at the Mount of Olives, he sent out two of his disciples.
And he said to the two of them, 'Go into the village that is in front of you; as soon as you enter you will find a young donkey tied there, which no one has ever ridden. Untie it and lead it here.'
'And if anyone asks you, "Why are you doing this?" say, "The Lord needs it, and he will send it back here immediately."'
The two of them went and found a young donkey tied at a door outside on the street, and they untied it.
Some people who were standing there asked them, 'What are you two doing, untying the young donkey?'
The two of them answered just as Jesus had told them, and those people let them take the donkey.
The two of them brought the young donkey to Jesus and draped their cloaks on the donkey's back, and he mounted on it (royal-register).
Many people spread their cloaks on the road, and others took leafy branches that they had cut from the fields and laid them down.
Those who walked ahead and those who followed behind all shouted, 'Hosanna! «May the one who comes in the name of the Lord be blessed!»'
'May the coming kingdom of David our ancestor be blessed! Hosanna in the highest place!'
He entered Jerusalem, went into the temple precincts, and after looking around at everything, since it was already late evening, he went out to Bethany with his twelve disciples.
On the next day, when they left Bethany, he was hungry.

When he saw from a distance a fig tree that had leaves, he went to see whether he would find anything on it, but when he reached it he found nothing except leaves, because it was not yet the season for figs.
So he said to that fig tree, 'From now on let no one ever eat fruit from you forever,' and his disciples heard it.

They then arrived at Jerusalem. When he went into the temple precincts, he began to drive out those buying and selling out of the temple, and he overturned the tables of the money-changers and the seats of those selling doves.
And he did not permit anyone to carry any vessel through the temple precincts at all.
He taught and said to them, 'Is it not written: «My house will be called a house of prayer for all the nations»? But you have made it «a den of robbers.»'
When the chief priests and the scribes heard, they sought a way to destroy him, because they feared him — for the crowd was amazed at his teaching.
When evening came, he and his disciples went outside the city.
In the morning, as they walked past, they saw the fig tree withered from the roots.
Peter remembered and said to him, 'Rabbi, look! The fig tree that you cursed has withered.'
Jesus answered them, 'Have faith in God.'
'Truly I tell you, whoever commands this mountain, "Be uprooted and thrown into the sea," and does not doubt in his heart but believes that what he says will happen, so it will be for him.'
'For this reason I tell you, whatever you pray and ask for, believe that you have already received it, and you will have that thing.'
'And when you stand praying, if you have any matter against anyone, forgive, so that your Father who dwells in heaven will also forgive your trespasses.'
They then arrived at Jerusalem again, and while he was walking in the temple precincts, the chief priests, the scribes, and the elders came to him.
They said to him, 'By what authority do you do these things? Or who gave you such authority to do these things?'
Jesus said to them, 'I will ask you one matter. Answer me, then I will tell you by what authority I do these things.'
'The baptism of John — was it from heaven or from men? Answer me!'
They discussed among themselves, 'What shall we say? If we say, "From heaven," he will ask, "Then why did you not believe John?"'
'But if we say, "From men"...' They feared the crowd, for everyone held that John really was a prophet.
So they answered Jesus, 'We do not know.' And Jesus said to them, 'Neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things.'
